title: Layout Skeleton
description: Learn semantic layout tags: header, main, footer, nav, section, and more.

levels.title: Page Skeleton
levels.learningPoint: Most web pages are divided into header, main, and footer
levels.instructions: Write header, main, and footer in the correct order.
levels.starterCode: <!-- Write header, main, footer in order -->
levels.hint: Just like reading top to bottom, pages are structured from top to bottom.
levels.successMessage: These three elements are the foundation of semantic HTML!

levels.title: Nav Placement
levels.learningPoint: Navigation typically goes inside the header
levels.instructions: Place a nav element inside the header.
levels.hint: Simply put a nav tag inside the header.
levels.successMessage: nav groups the main navigation links. Placing it inside header is the common pattern.

levels.title: Sections in Main
levels.learningPoint: Use section to group content by topic inside main
levels.instructions: Create 2 sections inside main, each with an h2 heading.
levels.hint: Each section should have its own h2 heading to identify the topic.
levels.successMessage: Giving each section a heading makes the document structure clear!

levels.title: Section vs Div
levels.learningPoint: section is a thematic grouping, div is a generic container
levels.instructions: A content block titled 'Latest News' with several paragraphs — which tag is more appropriate?
levels.context: A thematic content block with an h2 heading and multiple paragraphs
levels.hint: This block has a clear topic: 'Latest News'.
levels.successMessage: section is for thematic groupings that could have a heading. div has no semantic meaning.

levels.title: Aside Placement
levels.learningPoint: aside holds content indirectly related to the main content
levels.instructions: Place main and aside side by side. Put h1 in main and h2 in aside.
levels.hint: aside is used for sidebars, related links, and supplementary content.
levels.successMessage: aside is commonly used for blog sidebars, related articles, and ads!
